title: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.title: Understanding Aphasia: The Role of the Left Perisylvian Network
items.content: The production and comprehension of language are dependent on a distributed network in the left perisylvian region. Key areas include Broca's area, which affects verbal fluency and grammar, and Wernicke's area, traditionally linked to language comprehension. However, recent findings suggest that word comprehension involves the anterior temporal lobe more than Wernicke's area.
keyPoints: The left perisylvian network is crucial for language processing.
Broca's area (inferior frontal gyrus) affects verbal fluency and grammar.
Wernicke's area (temporoparietal junction) traditionally linked to comprehension; however, word comprehension involves the anterior temporal lobe more significantly.
Aphasia diagnosis requires deficits in formal language aspects like word finding or grammar.
~90% of right-handers and 60% of left-handers experience aphasia with left hemisphere lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.title: Diagnosing Aphasia: Key Considerations
items.content: Aphasia should be diagnosed based on deficits in formal language aspects such as word finding, repetition, and grammar. It is important to differentiate aphasia from other speech disorders like dysarthria or apraxia of speech, which do not qualify for an aphasia diagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of aphasia requires deficits in formal language aspects.
Dysarthria, apraxia of speech, and mutism are not sufficient for diagnosing aphasia.
Aphasia is prevalent in ~90% of right-handers and 60% of left-handers with left hemisphere lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.title: Assessment of Naming in Aphasic Patients
items.content: In the clinical examination of language, assessing naming (anomia) is crucial as it is the most common finding in aphasic patients. Anomias can manifest as failure to name an object, circumlocution, or incorrect word usage such as semantic or phonemic paraphasias.
keyPoints: Anomia is the single most common finding in aphasic patients.
Semantic paraphasia: Incorrect but related word (e.g., 'elevator' for 'escalator').
Phonemic paraphasia: Phonetically inaccurate word ('plentil' for 'pencil').
One-way naming deficit: Cannot retrieve name when shown an object, but can recognize it.
Two-way naming deficit: Cannot provide, define, or recognize the correct noun.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Characteristics of Spontaneous Speech in Aphasia
items.content: Spontaneous speech is evaluated for fluency and grammatical integrity. Fluent speech maintains appropriate volume, phrase length, and melody, while nonfluent speech is sparse and halting with average utterance lengths below four words.
keyPoints: Fluent speech: Maintains appropriate output volume, phrase length, and melody.
Nonfluent speech: Sparse, halting, and average utterance length is below four words.
Assess grammar through word order (syntax), tenses, suffixes, prefixes, plurals, and possessives.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Wernicke's Aphasia
items.content: Wernicke's aphasia is characterized by impaired comprehension for both spoken and written language, with fluent but nonsensical speech output. Patients produce paraphasic errors and neologisms, leading to jargon aphasia. This type of aphasia includes both expressive and receptive components, affecting repetition, naming, reading, and writing.
keyPoints: Comprehension is impaired for spoken and written words.
Speech is fluent but highly paraphasic and circumlocutious with neologisms.
Gestures do not improve communication; patients may be agitated or paranoid.
Lesion site: posterior portion of the language network, often due to embolus in the inferior division of MCA.
Prognosis for recovery is guarded.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Features and Management of Broca's Aphasia
items.content: Broca's aphasia presents with nonfluent, labored speech that is telegraphic but informative. Patients have difficulty with fluency, naming, and repetition, though comprehension remains intact for simple sentences. Neurological deficits often accompany this aphasia, including right facial weakness and hemiparesis.
keyPoints: Speech is nonfluent, labored, and telegraphic.
Comprehension of spoken language is intact except for complex syntax.
Lesion site: infarction of Broca's area due to occlusion of the superior division of MCA.
Recovery peaks within 2–6 months; speech therapy is more successful than in Wernicke's aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Differentiating Aphasia Types
items.content: Differentiating between Wernicke's and Broca's aphasia involves assessing speech fluency, comprehension, and associated neurological signs. Wernicke's aphasia is marked by fluent but nonsensical speech with poor comprehension, while Broca's aphasia features nonfluent, meaningful speech with preserved comprehension for simple sentences.
keyPoints: Wernicke's: Fluent speech, impaired comprehension, neologisms.
Broca's: Nonfluent speech, intact comprehension for simple sentences, right facial weakness.
Lesion sites differ: Wernicke's in posterior language network; Broca's in inferior frontal convolution.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.title: Conduction Aphasia: Characteristics and Lesion Localization
items.content: Conduction aphasia is characterized by fluent speech output with phonemic paraphasias, intact comprehension, severely impaired repetition, and preserved reading comprehension but impaired reading aloud. The lesion typically involves the temporoparietal or dorsal perisylvian region, interfering with the arcuate fasciculus.
keyPoints: Speech output is fluent with phonemic paraphasias
Comprehension of spoken language is intact
Repetition is severely impaired
Lesion usually involves temporoparietal or dorsal perisylvian region
Interferes with arcuate fasciculus function
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.title: Transcortical Aphasias: Fluent and Nonfluent Variants
items.content: Fluent transcortical aphasia resembles Wernicke's but with intact repetition, while nonfluent transcortical aphasia resembles Broca's with intact repetition. Lesions disconnect the language network from other brain areas, often involving watershed zones or specific cortical regions.
keyPoints: Fluent variant: Similar to Wernicke's with intact repetition
Nonfluent variant: Similar to Broca's with intact repetition and less grammatism
Lesions disconnect language network from other brain areas
Common causes include cerebrovascular lesions in watershed zones
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.title: Pure Word Deafness: Etiology and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Pure word deafness is caused by bilateral or left-sided MCA strokes affecting the superior temporal gyrus, disrupting auditory information flow to the language network. Patients understand written language but cannot process spoken language, often developing lipreading skills over time.
keyPoints: Causes: Bilateral or left-sided MCA strokes affecting superior temporal gyrus
Patients cannot process spoken language but understand written language
No difficulty with environmental sounds if right hemisphere is spared
Commonly leads to agitated paranoid reactions in acute stages
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Pure Alexia Without Agraphia
items.content: Pure alexia without agraphia, also known as 'word blindness,' results from lesions affecting the left occipital cortex and splenium of the corpus callosum. This disrupts visual input to the language network while preserving other language functions.
keyPoints: Lesions typically involve the left occipital cortex and posterior sector of the corpus callosum (splenium).
Results in right hemianopia.
Patients can understand, produce spoken language, name objects in the left visual field, repeat, and write.
Visual information from written words cannot reach the language network.
Common etiologies include vascular lesions in the posterior cerebral artery territory or neoplasms affecting the left occipital cortex and splenium.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.title: Color Anomia in Pure Alexia Without Agraphia
items.content: Patients with pure alexia without agraphia may experience color anomia, where they lose the ability to name colors but can match them. This occurs due to lesions affecting pathways involved in visual processing and language.
keyPoints: Color anomia is characterized by an inability to name colors while retaining the ability to match them.
Lesions often involve the posterior cerebral artery territory, which supplies medial temporal components of the limbic system.
Amnesia may accompany pure alexia but is usually transient due to unilateral limbic lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.title: Understanding Apraxia and Aphemia
items.content: Apraxia is a complex motor deficit not due to pyramidal, extrapyramidal, cerebellar, or sensory dysfunction. It involves difficulty in executing learned movements despite understanding the task. Apraxia of speech affects syllable articulation and can result from CVAs in Broca's area or FTLD with tauopathy. Aphemia presents as acute speech impairment (often mutism) but preserves writing, reading, and comprehension; it is not a true aphasia.
keyPoints: Apraxia involves complex motor deficits unrelated to pyramidal/extrapyramidal/cerebellar/sensory dysfunction.
Apraxia of speech affects syllable articulation and can result from CVAs in Broca's area or FTLD with tauopathy.
Aphemia presents as acute speech impairment (often mutism) but preserves writing, reading, and comprehension; it is not a true aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.title: Diagnosing Ideomotor Apraxia
items.content: Ideomotor apraxia involves difficulty performing specific motor acts or pantomiming tool use without the real object, despite understanding commands. It results from disconnection of language networks from pyramidal motor systems and is associated with lesions in the left hemisphere, often seen with Broca's aphasia.
keyPoints: Ideomotor apraxia involves difficulty performing specific motor acts or pantomiming tool use without the real object.
It results from disconnection of language networks from pyramidal motor systems.
Associated with lesions in the left hemisphere, often seen with Broca's aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Gerstmann's Syndrome
items.content: Gerstmann's syndrome includes acalculia, dysgraphia, finger anomia, and right-left confusion. It arises from damage to the inferior parietal lobule, particularly the angular gyrus in the left hemisphere. Diagnosis requires ruling out generalized anomia or aphasia.
keyPoints: Gerstmann's syndrome includes acalculia, dysgraphia, finger anomia, and right-left confusion.
Arises from damage to the inferior parietal lobule, particularly the angular gyrus in the left hemisphere.
Diagnosis requires ruling out generalized anomia or aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.title: Impact of Right Hemisphere Damage on Prosody
items.content: Damage to right hemisphere regions corresponding to Broca's area can impair the ability to introduce meaning-appropriate prosody into spoken language, a condition known as expressive aprosodia. This results in grammatically correct speech with accurate word choice but delivered in a monotone that may be misinterpreted as depression or indifference.
keyPoints: Prosody involves variations of intonation modifying the message conveyed.
Expressive aprosodia is characterized by monotone speech despite grammatical correctness.
Damage to right hemisphere regions corresponding to Broca's area affects prosody.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.title: Pragmatic Language Impairments from Right Hemisphere or Frontal Lobe Damage
items.content: Damage to the right hemisphere or frontal lobes impairs various aspects of pragmatics, including the ability to infer figurative language and emotions expressed through intonation and body language. This affects how patients understand and convey non-literal meanings in communication.
keyPoints: Pragmatics involves conveying attitude, affect, and figurative language.
Damage to right hemisphere or frontal lobes impairs pragmatic abilities.
Patients may struggle with understanding figurative language and emotional cues.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.title: Diagnosis and Subtypes of Primary Progressive Aphasia (PPA)
items.content: Primary Progressive Aphasia is diagnosed when a language disorder becomes the primary concern for 1–2 years, distinguishing it from other neurodegenerative syndromes. PPA can be caused by FTLD or Alzheimer's disease, with rare cases linked to Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of PPA is justified if aphasia is the primary concern for 1–2 years.
PPA can result from FTLD (tauopathy or TDP-43 proteinopathy) or Alzheimer's disease.
Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease can rarely cause a similar syndrome with rapid progression.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.title: Characteristics of Agrammatic PPA
items.content: Agrammatic PPA is marked by low fluency and impaired grammar, resembling Broca's aphasia but without right hemiparesis. It involves atrophy in Broca's area and adjacent frontal lobe regions.
keyPoints: Characterized by low fluency and impaired grammar with intact word comprehension.
Resembles Broca's aphasia but lacks right hemiparesis.
Peak atrophy sites include Broca's area and adjacent parts of the frontal lobe.
Neuropathology is usually FTLD with tauopathy or atypical AD.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Semantic PPA
items.content: Semantic PPA involves preserved fluency and syntax but poor single-word comprehension, with peak atrophy in the left anterior temporal lobe. It differs from Wernicke's aphasia due to informative speech and intact repetition.
keyPoints: Preserved fluency and syntax with poor single-word comprehension.
Profound two-way naming impairments are present.
Peak atrophy sites are in the left anterior temporal lobe.
Neuropathology is often FTLD with TDP-43 type C.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.title: Understanding Hemispatial Neglect: Right Hemisphere Lesions and Consequences
items.content: Hemispatial neglect often results from damage to the parieto-frontal network, particularly affecting the right hemisphere. This leads to severe contralesional left hemispatial neglect due to the lack of ipsilateral attentional mechanisms in the unaffected left hemisphere.
keyPoints: Right hemisphere lesions cause more common and severe contralesional neglect than left hemisphere lesions.
Neglect is characterized by failure to attend to stimuli on the contralateral side, often manifesting as inability to dress or eat from one side of the body.
Bedside tests like simultaneous bilateral stimulation reveal extinction phenomena in patients with right hemisphere damage.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.title: Clinical Examination and Diagnosis of Hemispatial Neglect
items.content: Patients with hemispatial neglect exhibit specific behaviors such as failing to groom or eat from the neglected side. Diagnostic tests include simultaneous bilateral stimulation and visual target cancellation, which help identify sensory-representational and exploratory deficits.
keyPoints: Simultaneous bilateral stimulation can reveal extinction in patients with right hemisphere injury.
Visual target cancellation test involves detecting targets on a sheet of paper; failure to detect left-side targets indicates neglect.
Neglect is not due to hemianopia alone, as patients can turn their head and eyes to the neglected side.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.title: Anosognosia in Hemispatial Neglect: Denial of Deficits
items.content: Some patients with hemispatial neglect may exhibit anosognosia, denying the existence of their hemiparesis or even ownership of a paralyzed limb. This condition is rarely seen in neurodegenerative diseases that selectively atrophy components of the right hemisphere attentional network.
keyPoints: Anosognosia involves denial of hemiparesis and can include denial of limb ownership.
Hemispatial neglect with anosognosia is uncommon in neurodegenerative diseases affecting the right hemisphere.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.title: Diagnosis of Simultanagnosia Using Modified Letter Cancellation Task
items.content: Simultanagnosia can be diagnosed bedside using a modified letter cancellation task where larger targets are embedded among foils. Patients with simultanagnosia often miss these larger targets due to their inability to integrate visual information across multiple fixation points.
keyPoints: Simultanagnosia involves an inability to integrate central and peripheral visual information.
Modified letter cancellation task: Larger targets (7.5–10 cm vs 2.5 cm) are used.
Patients with simultanagnosia miss larger targets, indicating a central rather than peripheral problem.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.title: Characteristics and Causes of Bálint's Syndrome
items.content: Bálint's syndrome results from bilateral parietal lesions affecting spatial attention. It includes oculomotor apraxia, optic ataxia, and simultanagnosia. Common causes include CVAs, head injuries, and neoplasms in the right hemisphere.
keyPoints: Bálint's syndrome involves deficits in visuomotor scanning, manual reaching, and visual integration.
Causes: CVAs, head injury, neoplasms (right hemisphere), watershed infarction, hypoglycemia, sagittal sinus thrombosis.
Improvement of hemispatial neglect is common within weeks post-CVA.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.title: Posterior Cortical Atrophy and Related Cognitive Disorders
items.content: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is a variant of Alzheimer's disease characterized by neurofibrillary degeneration in the parieto-occipital cortex. It presents with progressive spatial disorientation, hemispatial neglect, Balint's syndrome, and apraxias.
keyPoints: PCA represents an atypical presentation of AD with neurofibrillary degeneration in specific brain areas.
Symptoms include progressive hemispatial neglect, Balint's syndrome, dressing and construction apraxia.
Other causes: Lewy body disease (LBD), CJD, FTLD (corticobasal type).
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.title: Understanding Prosopagnosia and Its Distinction from Other Agnosias
items.content: Prosopagnosia, or face blindness, is characterized by an inability to recognize familiar faces despite intact perceptual abilities. Patients can distinguish between different faces but cannot identify specific individuals. This condition results from lesions in the ventral temporal lobe and does not affect the ability to recognize a face as a face or other generic object identifications.
keyPoints: Prosopagnosia involves modality-specific deficits, allowing recognition through auditory cues.
Lesions are typically located in the ventral temporal lobe.
Patients can identify objects generically but struggle with unique features of individual faces or items.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.title: Differential Diagnosis: Semantic Dementia vs. Semantic PPA
items.content: Semantic dementia and semantic PPA both involve deficits in word comprehension and object recognition but differ in their preservation of face and object recognition abilities. Semantic dementia, often associated with FTLD-TDP type C or Pick's disease, leads to a loss of understanding the meaning of words denoting objects and recognizing faces and objects. In contrast, semantic PPA primarily affects word comprehension and naming while preserving some recognition capabilities.
keyPoints: Semantic dementia involves bilateral or right-sided infarctions affecting the fusiform gyrus.
Associated deficits may include visual field defects and achromatopsia.
Acute onset of semantic dementia can be linked to herpes simplex encephalitis.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.title: Diagnosis of Amnesic State: Bilateral Limbic Network Damage
items.content: A persistent global amnestic state is diagnosed when there are no deficits in motivation, attention, language, or visuospatial function but with bilateral damage to the limbic network, typically within the hippocampus-entorhinal complex or thalamus.
keyPoints: Persistent global amnestic state requires bilateral damage to the limbic network.
Commonly affects the hippocampus-entorhinal complex or thalamus.
Amnesic states involve multimodal memory disturbance with retrograde and anterograde components.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.title: Characteristics of Anterograde Amnesia in Limbic Network Damage
items.content: Anterograde amnesia, a key feature of limbic network damage, involves the inability to store, retain, and recall new knowledge. Patients may confabulate to fill memory gaps, especially if frontothalamic networks are also affected.
keyPoints: Anterograde amnesia indicates an inability to form new memories.
Patients often confabulate when experiencing anterograde amnesia.
Confabulation is more common with additional frontothalamic network damage, such as in Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome or traumatic head injury.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.title: Testing Anterograde Amnesia in Clinical Examination
items.content: To assess anterograde amnesia, present a list of four to five words read aloud up to five times or until the patient can repeat them immediately. After engaging the patient in other tasks for 5–10 minutes, test recall. Failure indicates significant memory impairment.
keyPoints: List of four to five words
Read aloud up to five times
Immediate repetition without delay
Recall phase after 5–10 min
Failure in recall suggests severe anterograde amnesia
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.title: Differentiating Memory Impairments: Amnestic State vs. Confusional States
items.content: Amnestic states primarily affect memory encoding and retrieval with bilateral limbic lesions, whereas confusional states from toxic-metabolic encephalopathies also impair attention-related tasks.
keyPoints: Bilateral limbic lesions in amnestic states
Attention impairment differentiates confusional states
Encoding and retrieval affected in both
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.title: Characteristics of Transient Global Amnesia (TGA)
items.content: TGA presents with acute disorientation and anterograde amnesia lasting 24–48 hours. Retrograde amnesia for recent events occurs before onset. Recurrences happen in ~20% of patients, often without a specific etiology.
keyPoints: Acute disorientation
Anterograde amnesia
Duration: 24–48 h
Retrograde amnesia for recent events
Recurrence rate: ~20%
Possible causes: migraine, autonomic activation, seizures
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.title: Understanding Frontal Lobe Syndromes: Differentiating bvFTD and Frontal Disinhibition Syndrome
items.content: The frontal lobes, particularly the dorsolateral, medial, and orbitofrontal components, are crucial for executive functions. In behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD), pathology in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex leads to impaired social conduct and empathy. Conversely, dorsolateral dysfunction affects information processing and working memory. Frontal disinhibition syndrome is characterized by a lack of judgment and foresight despite intact intellectual function.
keyPoints: Frontal lobe syndromes involve the dorsolateral, medial, and orbitofrontal prefrontal cortex components.
bvFTD pathology in ventromedial prefrontal cortex leads to impaired social conduct and empathy.
Dorsolateral dysfunction affects information processing speed, working memory, and executive functions.
Frontal disinhibition syndrome shows preserved intellectual function but lacks judgment, insight, foresight, and adherence to social rules.
Behavioral impairments may not be evident in structured settings like a medical office.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.title: Diagnosing Frontal Lobe Disease: The Role of Historical Information
items.content: Frontal lobe diseases, such as frontal abulic and disinhibition syndromes, may not be apparent during structured clinical evaluations. Patients can display inappropriate behaviors in real-life situations despite appearing normal in a medical office setting. Therefore, clinicians must rely on historical information to diagnose these conditions when the mental state appears intact.
keyPoints: Frontal abulic syndrome involves loss of initiative and emotional blandness.
Frontal disinhibition syndrome is marked by social disinhibition and poor judgment.
Behavioral impairments may not manifest in structured environments like medical offices.
Diagnosis often relies on historical information due to the discrepancy between clinical examination and real-life behavior.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.title: Frontal Release Signs in Structural Lesions and Metabolic Encephalopathies
items.content: Developmentally primitive reflexes like grasping and sucking are indicative of large structural lesions extending into the premotor components of the frontal lobes or metabolic encephalopathies. These signs are not commonly present in patients with prefrontal lesions and behavioral syndromes.
keyPoints: Frontal release signs include grasping (elicited by stroking the palm) and sucking (elicited by stroking the lips).
Seen primarily in large structural lesions extending into premotor components of frontal lobes or metabolic encephalopathies.
Not commonly present in patients with prefrontal lesions and behavioral syndromes.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Assessment of Working Memory and Attention in Frontal Lobe Damage
items.content: Frontal lobe damage impairs attention-related functions, including working memory, concentration span, retrieval of stored information, inhibition of inappropriate responses, and mental flexibility. Specific tests reveal decreased digit span (average 7 forward, 5 reverse), slowed recitation of months in reverse (<15 s), reduced word fluency (≥12 per letter), inability to inhibit 'no go' stimuli, impaired mental flexibility, increased distractibility, and difficulty with abstracting similarities.
keyPoints: Digit span is decreased: average 7 forward, 5 reverse.
Recitation of months in reverse should take <15 s; slowed indicates poor working memory.
Word fluency test (≥12 words per letter a, f, or s in 1 min) shows diminished ability to retrieve information.
'Go-no go' tasks reveal inability to inhibit response to 'no go' stimulus.
Mental flexibility is impaired, with increased distractibility and tendency for impersistence/perseverance.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.title: Frontal Lobe Syndromes and Their Causes
items.content: Frontal lobe syndromes can arise from various causes including trauma, neoplasms, and neurodegenerative diseases. The type of behavioral syndrome (abulic/apathetic vs. disinhibition) is linked to specific cortical damage locations.
keyPoints: Abulic/apathetic syndrome associated with dorsolateral or dorsomedial prefrontal cortex damage.
Disinhibition syndrome linked to orbitofrontal or ventromedial cortex damage, often right-sided in asymmetric disease.
Common causes: head trauma, ruptured aneurysms, hydrocephalus, tumors (metastases, glioblastoma, meningioma), and focal neurodegenerations like Pick's disease and FTLD.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.title: Behavioral Changes in bvFTD and Associated Atrophy Patterns
items.content: Behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD) presents with diverse behavioral changes due to atrophy in specific brain regions. It can manifest as apathy, disinhibition, or compulsive behaviors.
keyPoints: Atrophy includes orbitofrontal cortex, anterior temporal lobe, insula, and anterior cingulate cortex.
Behavioral changes range from apathy to inappropriate social behavior, compulsions, and dietary alterations.
Rarely associated with atypical AD pathology showing neurofibrillary tangles in the prefrontal cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.question: Which area is traditionally associated with language comprehension based on stroke patient examinations?
items.explanation: Wernicke's area is traditionally associated with language comprehension based on stroke patient examinations.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.question: Damage to which region impairs verbal fluency and the grammatical structure of sentences?
items.explanation: Damage to the inferior frontal gyrus, known as Broca's area, impairs verbal fluency and grammatical structure.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of diagnosing aphasia?
items.explanation: Aphasia is diagnosed by deficits in formal aspects of language, not by dysarthria alone.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.question: Which type of paraphasia involves providing an incorrect but related word?
items.explanation: A semantic paraphasia occurs when a patient provides an incorrect but related word.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: What is the term for a deficit where a patient can neither provide, define, nor recognize the correct noun?
items.explanation: A two-way (comprehension-based or semantic) naming deficit exists if the patient can neither provide, define, nor recognize the correct noun.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: Which type of aphasia is rarely encountered in neurodegenerative diseases affecting Wernicke's area?
items.explanation: The full syndrome of Wernicke's aphasia is rarely, if ever, encountered in neurodegenerative diseases affecting Wernicke's area.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: Which type of aphasia is characterized by fluent but paraphasic and circumlocutious language output?
items.explanation: Wernicke's aphasia is characterized by fluent but paraphasic and circumlocutious language output.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.question: In which type of aphasia are gestures and pantomime ineffective in improving communication?
items.explanation: In Wernicke's aphasia, gestures and pantomime do not improve communication.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common neurologic deficit associated with Broca's aphasia?
items.explanation: Broca's aphasia is commonly associated with right facial weakness.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.question: Which artery is most commonly involved in causing Wernicke's aphasia due to cerebrovascular accidents?
items.explanation: The middle cerebral artery, particularly its inferior division, is most commonly involved in causing Wernicke's aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.question: Which aphasia is characterized by fluent speech with phonemic paraphasias and severely impaired repetition?
items.explanation: Conduction aphasia is characterized by fluent speech with phonemic paraphasias and severely impaired repetition.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.question: In which type of aphasia does the lesion disconnect the intact core language network from other temporoparietal association areas, while preserving repetition?
items.explanation: Fluent transcortical aphasia involves a lesion that disconnects the intact core language network from other temporoparietal association areas, preserving repetition.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.question: Which aphasia results in nonfluent speech output with impaired comprehension and is often associated with right hemiplegia?
items.explanation: Global aphasia results in nonfluent speech output with severely impaired comprehension and is often associated with right hemiplegia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.question: What type of aphasia involves intact articulation, comprehension, and repetition but impaired confrontation naming and word finding?
items.explanation: Anomic aphasia involves intact articulation, comprehension, and repetition but impaired confrontation naming and word finding.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best describes the typical visual field defect associated with pure alexia without agraphia?
items.explanation: Pure alexia without agraphia is typically associated with right homonymous hemianopia due to damage in the left occipital cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.question: What is preserved in patients with pure alexia without agraphia?
options: Naming objects in the right visual hemifield
items.explanation: Patients can understand and produce spoken language despite their inability to read.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following pathways is disrupted in pure alexia without agraphia, leading to reading difficulties?
options: Transcallosal pathways anterior to the splenium
items.explanation: The disruption of transcallosal pathways posterior to the splenium prevents visual information from reaching the language network.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.question: Which type of apraxia is characterized by a deficit in sequencing goal-directed movements?
items.explanation: Ideational apraxia involves a deficit in the sequencing of goal-directed movements.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by acalculia, dysgraphia, finger anomia, and right-left confusion?
items.explanation: Gerstmann's syndrome includes acalculia, dysgraphia, finger anomia, and right-left confusion.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.question: What is the typical recovery pattern for aphemia?
options: Intermediate stage of hoarse whispering before recovery
items.explanation: Aphemia typically involves an intermediate stage of hoarse whispering before recovery.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.question: Where is the lesion site that can cause ideomotor limb apraxia?
options: Medial aspects of the frontal lobes
items.explanation: Ideomotor limb apraxia can be caused by lesions on the medial aspects of the frontal lobes.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.question: Which area of the brain is associated with impairments in expressive aprosodia?
options: Right hemisphere regions corresponding to Broca's area
Left hemisphere regions corresponding to Wernicke's area
items.explanation: Damage to right hemisphere regions corresponding to Broca's area impairs the ability to introduce meaning-appropriate prosody into spoken language.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.question: What aspect of language is affected by expressive aprosodia?
items.explanation: Expressive aprosodia affects prosody, which includes variations in intonation that modify the nature of the message.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.question: Which hemisphere is primarily involved in impairments affecting the ability to infer figurative language?
items.explanation: Damage to the right hemisphere or frontal lobes impairs the ability to infer the figurative aspect of a message.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.question: Which subtype of Progressive Primary Aphasia (PPA) is characterized by consistently low fluency and impaired grammar but intact word comprehension?
items.explanation: The agrammatic variant of PPA is characterized by low fluency and impaired grammar but intact word comprehension.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.question: What is the most common neuropathology associated with Logopenic PPA?
items.explanation: Logopenic PPA is most commonly associated with the pathology of Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.question: Which area shows peak atrophy in Semantic PPA?
items.explanation: The left anterior temporal lobe shows peak atrophy in Semantic PPA.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.question: Which subtype of PPA is characterized by preserved fluency and syntax but poor single-word comprehension?
items.explanation: The semantic variant of PPA is characterized by preserved fluency and syntax but poor single-word comprehension.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.question: Which cortical component of the parietofrontal network is primarily responsible for providing motor strategies for attentional behaviors?
items.explanation: The frontal eye fields are responsible for motor strategies for attentional behaviors.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.question: What is the primary reason that left hemisphere lesions do not typically result in severe contralesional neglect?
options: The left hemisphere does not contribute to spatial orientation.
The right hemisphere can compensate for the loss of contralaterally directed attentional functions.
Left hemisphere lesions rarely affect spatial cognition.
The striatum compensates for left hemisphere damage.
items.explanation: The right hemisphere's global attentional mechanisms can compensate for the loss of the left hemisphere's contralaterally directed attentional functions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.question: What phenomenon occurs when a patient with right hemisphere injury perceives bilaterally presented stimuli as coming only from the right?
items.explanation: Extinction is when bilaterally presented stimuli are perceived as coming only from the right after a right hemisphere injury.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.question: Which of the following conditions is almost never seen in neurodegenerative diseases that cause selective atrophy within components of the right hemisphere attentional network?
items.explanation: Hemispatial neglect is almost never seen in neurodegenerative diseases affecting the right hemisphere attentional network.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of simultanagnosia?
options: Patients can integrate visual information across multiple fixation points.
Patients have difficulty with oculomotor apraxia and optic ataxia.
Patients may miss larger targets in a modified letter cancellation task.
Patients show improvement within the first several weeks.
items.explanation: Simultanagnosia is characterized by patients missing larger targets due to difficulty integrating visual information across multiple fixation points.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.question: What is a common cause of hemispatial neglect?
options: Cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs) in the right hemisphere.
items.explanation: Hemispatial neglect is commonly caused by CVAs, head injury, and neoplasms in the right hemisphere.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.question: Which condition is associated with a severe difficulty in copying simple line drawings?
items.explanation: Construction apraxia involves severe difficulties in copying simple line drawings, especially with right hemisphere lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by progressive spatial disorientation and may represent a variant of Alzheimer's disease (AD)?
items.explanation: Posterior cortical atrophy (PCA) is a progressive form of spatial disorientation and can be an atypical presentation of AD.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.question: Which brain region is primarily associated with prosopagnosia?
items.explanation: The ventral temporal lobe is the region associated with prosopagnosia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.question: What type of agnosia involves difficulty in recognizing the identity of individual faces or objects?
items.explanation: Associative prosopagnosia involves difficulty in recognizing the identity of individual faces or objects.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.question: Which condition is characterized by a deficit that extends to recognizing objects by their characteristic sound or vocalization?
items.explanation: Multimodal object agnosia is characterized by a deficit that extends to recognizing objects by their characteristic sound or vocalization.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.question: Which area is primarily responsible for declarative (explicit) memory within the limbic network?
options: Hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, posterior paralimbic areas, and limbic nuclei of the thalamus
Anterior and medial nuclei of the thalamus alone
items.explanation: The hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, posterior paralimbic areas, and limbic nuclei of the thalamus are responsible for declarative memory.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.question: What is a common feature of anterograde amnesia?
options: Inability to recall past events before the onset of the condition
Loss of motor skills and perceptual abilities
Inability to store, retain, and recall new knowledge
Complete loss of all memory functions
items.explanation: Anterograde amnesia is characterized by an inability to store, retain, and recall new information.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.question: Which of the following statements about confabulation in amnestic states is true?
options: Confabulation involves accurate recollection of past events.
It occurs due to damage solely within the hippocampus-entorhinal complex.
Patients with confabulation often fill memory gaps with fabricated information.
Confabulation only appears in patients without any limbic network damage.
items.explanation: Confabulation involves filling memory gaps with inaccurate, fabricated information, especially when the frontothalamic network is affected.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.question: Which component of an amnestic state is usually more prominent?
items.explanation: The anterograde component of amnestic states is usually much more prominent than the retrograde component.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.question: What indicates a less severe memory disturbance in an amnestic patient?
options: Failure to recall words after a delay
Inability to recognize words by multiple choice
Accurate recognition of words by multiple choice despite inability to recall them
items.explanation: Accurate recognition of the words by multiple choice in a patient who cannot recall them indicates a less severe memory disturbance that affects mostly the retrieval stage of memory.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with progressive memory impairments in the elderly?
items.explanation: The most common cause of progressive memory impairments in the elderly is Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.question: What characterizes transient global amnesia?
options: Persistent loss of all memory types
Inability to retain new information and retrograde amnesia for recent events
items.explanation: Transient global amnesia is characterized by anterograde amnesia (inability to retain new information) and a retrograde amnesia for relatively recent events that occurred before the onset.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: Which components of the frontal lobes are included in the term 'frontal lobe syndrome'?
options: Motor-premotor, dorsolateral prefrontal, medial prefrontal, and orbitofrontal components
Dorsolateral prefrontal, medial prefrontal, and orbitofrontal components
items.explanation: Frontal lobe syndrome refers to the dorsolateral prefrontal, medial prefrontal, and orbitofrontal components.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: What aspect of cognitive function is primarily associated with the dorsolateral parts of the prefrontal cortex?
options: Speed of information processing, working memory, and executive functions
items.explanation: The dorsolateral parts are related to the speed of information processing, working memory, and other aspects of executive functions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by a loss of initiative, creativity, curiosity, emotional blandness, apathy, and lack of empathy?
items.explanation: The frontal abulic syndrome is characterized by these features.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.question: Which reflex is elicited by stroking the lips in patients with large structural lesions extending into the premotor components of the frontal lobes?
items.explanation: The sucking reflex is elicited by stroking the lips in patients with large structural lesions extending into the premotor components of the frontal lobes.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: Which cognitive function is tested by having a patient recite the months of the year in reverse order?
items.explanation: Reciting the months of the year in reverse tests working memory, as it reflects the transient online holding and manipulation of information.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: What is indicated by a patient's diminished ability to produce words starting with specific letters within 1 minute?
items.explanation: Diminished fluency in producing words indicates an impairment in the ability to search and retrieve information from long-term stores, not just working memory.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.question: Which area of the brain is most commonly associated with behavioral disinhibition when there is asymmetric disease?
items.explanation: Behavioral disinhibition with asymmetric disease is typically associated with lesions predominantly on the right side of the orbitofrontal or ventromedial cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by progressive isolated impairment of comportment associated with degenerative diseases?
items.explanation: The bvFTD (behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia) syndrome is known for progressive isolated impairment of comportment associated with degenerative diseases.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.question: Which condition can cause a frontal lobe syndrome due to bilateral multifocal lesions that interfere with prefrontal cortex connectivity?
items.explanation: Bilateral multifocal lesions can interfere with prefrontal cortex connectivity, leading to a frontal lobe syndrome in conditions like metabolic encephalopathy, multiple sclerosis, and vitamin B12 deficiency.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.statement: Sentence comprehension is solely dependent on Wernicke's area.
items.explanation: Sentence comprehension involves both Broca's and Wernicke's areas, not just Wernicke's.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA
items.statement: In the majority of right-handers, aphasia occurs after lesions in the left hemisphere.
items.explanation: ~90% of right-handers experience aphasia after left hemisphere lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · Aphasia, Memory Loss, and Other Cognitive Disorders | THE LEFT PERISYLVIAN NETWORK FOR LANGUAGE AND APHASIAS IN STROKE AND DEMENTIA

items.statement: A patient with a one-way naming deficit can point to the appropriate object when its name is provided.
items.explanation: In most anomias, the patient cannot retrieve the appropriate name but can point to the correct object when named by the examiner.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Repetition testing with tongue twisters is a better assessment of aphasia than dysarthria.
items.explanation: Testing repetition with tongue twisters provides a better assessment of dysarthria and apraxia of speech rather than aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Patients with Broca's aphasia have impaired comprehension of spoken language.
items.explanation: Broca's aphasia includes a deficit in decoding syntax, affecting comprehension of complex sentences.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.statement: Wernicke's aphasia is primarily an expressive disorder without receptive components.
items.explanation: Wernicke's aphasia has both expressive and receptive language deficits.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.statement: In pure word deafness, patients cannot understand spoken language but can comprehend written language.
items.explanation: Patients with pure word deafness have difficulty understanding spoken language due to disruption in the auditory association cortex's connection to the language network, while comprehension of written language remains intact.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.statement: Isolation aphasia involves severe impairment in both speech production and repetition.
items.explanation: In isolation aphasia, comprehension is severely impaired, but the patient can parrot fragments of heard conversations, indicating that repetition mechanisms are partially intact.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with pure alexia without agraphia can accurately name objects in their left visual hemifield.
items.explanation: Objects in the left hemifield may be named because they activate nonvisual associations in the right hemisphere.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.statement: Pure alexia without agraphia is most commonly caused by lesions affecting the anterior cerebral artery.
items.explanation: The condition is usually due to vascular lesions in the territory of the posterior cerebral artery.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.statement: Writing, reading, and comprehension are impaired in aphemia.
items.explanation: In aphemia, writing, reading, and comprehension remain intact.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.statement: Gerstmann's syndrome is commonly associated with damage to the inferior parietal lobule in the left hemisphere.
items.explanation: Gerstmann's syndrome often arises from damage to the inferior parietal lobule, especially the angular gyrus, in the left hemisphere.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.statement: Prosody involves variations in word choice and syntax.
items.explanation: Prosody refers to variations of intonation, not word choice or syntax.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.statement: Damage to the right hemisphere can impair the ability to understand figurative language.
items.explanation: The passage states that damage to the right hemisphere affects the ability to infer the figurative aspect of a message.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 5)

items.statement: Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease can cause a syndrome identical to PPA with rapid progression.
items.explanation: CJD can rarely cause an identical syndrome to PPA but progresses more rapidly.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.statement: The comprehension impairment in Logopenic PPA is similar to that seen in Wernicke's aphasia.
items.explanation: Logopenic PPA lacks the comprehension impairment of Wernicke's aphasia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.statement: Hemianopia alone is sufficient to cause target detection failure in hemispatial neglect.
items.explanation: Target detection failures reflect a distortion of spatial attention, not just sensory input like hemianopia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.statement: Severe neglect for the right hemispace is common in left-handers with left hemisphere lesions.
items.explanation: Severe neglect for the right hemispace is rare, even in left-handers with left hemisphere lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.statement: Simultanagnosia can occur without the other components of Bálint’s syndrome.
items.explanation: Simultanagnosia may occur independently, especially in association with posterior cortical atrophy.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.statement: Patients with Bálint's syndrome always show improvement within the first several weeks.
items.explanation: Improvement is more typical for hemispatial neglect rather than Bálint’s syndrome.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.statement: Prosopagnosia is a perceptual deficit.
items.explanation: Prosopagnosia is not a perceptual deficit; it reflects the inability to activate multimodal associations necessary for concept formation.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA
items.statement: Semantic dementia can result from herpes simplex encephalitis.
items.explanation: Acute onset of semantic dementia syndrome can be associated with herpes simplex encephalitis.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE OCCIPITOTEMPORAL NETWORK FOR FACE AND OBJECT RECOGNITION | CAUSES AND RELATION TO SEMANTIC DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.statement: Damage to the limbic network results in the complete destruction of memories.
items.explanation: Damage interferes with conscious recall but does not destroy memories; implicit memory remains intact.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA
items.statement: Retrograde amnesia affects recent events more severely than remote ones.
items.explanation: Recent events are more vulnerable to retrograde amnesia due to less extensive consolidation.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · LIMBIC NETWORK FOR EXPLICIT MEMORY AND AMNESIA

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.statement: Unilateral left-sided hippocampal lesions typically result in a permanent amnestic state.
items.explanation: Occasionally, unilateral left-sided hippocampal lesions can give rise to an amnestic state, but the memory disorder tends to be transient.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.statement: Transient global amnesia is often followed by a complete recovery with no residual effects.
items.explanation: There is persistent loss of memory for the events that occurred during the ictus in transient global amnesia.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.statement: Impairments of social conduct and empathy in neurodegenerative frontal dementias are attributed to pathology of the dorsolateral parts of the prefrontal cortex.
items.explanation: These impairments are due to pathology of the ventromedial parts of the prefrontal cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.subtopic: THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR
items.statement: The frontal disinhibition syndrome can be characterized by severe impairments in judgment, insight, and foresight.
items.explanation: This syndrome involves social disinhibition and impairments of judgment, insight, and foresight.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PREFRONTAL NETWORK FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION AND BEHAVIOR

items.statement: Patients with prefrontal lesions typically display frontal release signs.
items.explanation: The majority of patients with prefrontal lesions do not display frontal release signs.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Severely amnestic patients may have intact working memory capacity.
items.explanation: Severely amnestic patients, such as those with Alzheimer's disease, can have intact or even superior working memory despite their inability to remember recent events.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.statement: The abulic/apathetic syndrome is associated with damage in the orbitofrontal or ventromedial cortex.
items.explanation: The abulic/apathetic syndrome tends to be associated with damage in the dorsolateral or dorsomedial prefrontal cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.subtopic: CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA
items.statement: Lesions in the caudate nucleus can produce a frontal lobe syndrome affecting executive functions.
items.explanation: Lesions in the caudate nucleus or dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus, as subcortical components of the prefrontal network, can indeed affect executive functions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CAUSES: TRAUMA, NEOPLASM, AND FRONTOTEMPORAL DEMENTIA

items.assertion: Patients with Wernicke's aphasia often have an unremarkable neurologic examination.
items.reason: The paraphasic, neologistic speech in these patients may lead to suspicion of a primary psychiatric disorder.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason explains why Wernicke's aphasia might be confused with psychiatric disorders.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Recovery of language function in Broca's aphasia generally peaks within 2–6 months.
items.reason: This is because the lesion site for Broca's aphasia is typically in the posterior portion of the language network.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; the lesion site for Broca's aphasia is in the anterior perisylvian and insular cortex.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 1)

items.assertion: Conduction aphasia results from a lesion in the temporoparietal or dorsal perisylvian region.
items.reason: The lesion interferes with the function of the arcuate fasciculus, which interconnects Broca's and Wernicke's areas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why conduction aphasia results from a lesion in that region.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.assertion: Nonfluent transcortical aphasia is characterized by impaired repetition.
items.reason: The lesion site disconnects the intact language network from prefrontal areas of the brain.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because nonfluent transcortical aphasia has intact repetition, while the reason is true regarding the disconnection from prefrontal areas.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 2)

items.assertion: Pure alexia without agraphia involves an inability to read.
items.reason: The visual information from written words cannot reach the language network due to damage in the left occipital cortex and splenium of the corpus callosum.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 3)

items.assertion: Ideomotor apraxia causes major limitations of daily living activities.
items.reason: Handling real objects is not impaired in ideomotor apraxia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because handling real objects is intact, so ideomotor apraxia does not cause major limitations.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.reason: Lesions of the anterior corpus callosum can result in ideomotor apraxia confined to one side.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · STROKE APHASIAS (part 4)

items.assertion: The agrammatic variant of PPA is most closely related to Broca's aphasia.
items.reason: It features low fluency and impaired grammar similar to Broca's aphasia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.assertion: Semantic PPA is characterized by impaired repetition.
items.reason: The comprehension deficit in Semantic PPA is more severe than in stroke-related posterior transcortical aphasia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Semantic PPA does not have impaired repetition, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · NEURODEGENERATIVE PROGRESSIVE APHASIAS

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.assertion: Right hemisphere lesions result in severe contralesional left hemispatial neglect.
items.reason: The unaffected left hemisphere does not contain ipsilateral attentional mechanisms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why right hemisphere lesions cause severe neglect.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT
items.assertion: Hemispatial neglect is a common outcome of left hemisphere damage.
items.reason: The right hemisphere cannot compensate for the loss of contralaterally directed attentional functions when the left hemisphere is damaged.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hemispatial neglect is more severe and common with right hemisphere damage; the reason is true.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · THE PARIETOFRONTAL NETWORK FOR SPATIAL ORIENTATION | HEMISPATIAL NEGLECT

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.assertion: Patients with posterior cortical atrophy display progressive hemispatial neglect.
items.reason: Posterior cortical atrophy is a variant of Alzheimer's disease with neurofibrillary degeneration in the parieto-occipital cortex.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the progressive hemispatial neglect seen in PCA.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS
items.assertion: Dressing apraxia is caused by lesions in the frontal cortex.
items.reason: Lesions that involve the posterior parietal cortex lead to dressing apraxia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; dressing apraxia results from bilateral parietal lesions, not frontal cortex lesions.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · BÁLINT'S SYNDROME, SIMULTANAGNOSIA, DRESSING APRAXIA, CONSTRUCTION APRAXIA, AND ROUTE-FINDING IMPAIRMENTS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.assertion: The entorhinal cortex and hippocampus are early sites of neurofibrillary degeneration in Alzheimer's disease.
items.reason: This is responsible for the initially selective impairment of episodic memory.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why there is an initial selective impairment of episodic memory.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
items.assertion: Confusional states caused by toxic-metabolic encephalopathies lead to amnestic states.
items.reason: These conditions primarily affect the limbic system causing memory impairments.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because confusional states from these causes lead to secondary memory impairments due to additional impairments in attention-related tasks, not solely affecting the limbic system.
items.reference: Aphasia Memory Loss and Other Cognitive Disorders · CLINICAL EXAMINATION | CAUSES, INCLUDING ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
